county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Haines Borough organized 1968 in northern Southeast Alaska at the head of Lynn Canal. Tlingit name: Dei Shu. Home to Chilkat and Chilkoot Tlingit peoples. Site of Fort William H. Seward (Chilkoot Barracks, 1904-1946). Connected to the Alaska Highway via the Haines Highway (1943). Fishing, timber, and tourism are primary industries.
county_metadata.research_tips: Haines Borough sits at the northern end of the Lynn Canal in Southeast Alaska. Tlingit peoples (Chilkat and Chilkoot) have occupied this area for millennia. Presbyterian mission records from 1881 (Haines Mission/Fort William Seward) are at the Presbyterian Historical Society in Philadelphia. Alaska State Archives holds territorial vital records from 1913 and First Judicial District court records. The Sheldon Museum and Cultural Center in Haines holds local family histories, photographs, fishing and mining records, and Dalton Trail gold rush materials. Fort William H. Seward (Chilkoot Barracks, 1904-1946) military personnel records are at NARA St. Louis. Tlingit clan and family records may be at the Sealaska Heritage Institute in Juneau and the Chilkat Indian Village tribal office. Federal census from 1880 for Haines area. BLM GLO land patents searchable online. The Haines Borough Clerk's office has borough-era administrative records from 1968. Haines area newspapers (Chilkat Valley News) at the Alaska State Library.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM GLO Records online -- federal land patents, homestead entries, and mining claims for Haines area; Alaska DNR Recorder's Office for recorded documents

source_profiles.source_name: Haines Territorial and State Vital Records -- Alaska State Archives / Bureau of Vital Statistics
source_profiles.repository: Alaska State Archives, Juneau / Alaska Bureau of Vital Statistics
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Territorial birth and death registrations for Haines area from 1913; marriage records from territorial period; modern vital records from the Alaska Bureau of Vital Statistics. Pre-1913 vital events must be traced through church records, Russian Orthodox parish registers, and missionary records
source_profiles.survival_notes: Territorial records generally complete from 1913; earlier coverage varies greatly by community

source_profiles.source_name: Haines Probate and Court Records -- First Judicial District Superior Court
source_profiles.repository: Alaska Superior Court, First Judicial District / Alaska State Archives, Juneau
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Superior Court handles probate (wills, estates, guardianships), adoption, and civil cases for Haines. First Judicial District records. Older territorial court files transferred to Alaska State Archives. Commissioner's court records from the territorial period may predate borough organization

source_profiles.repository: NARA / FamilySearch / Ancestry
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal census for Haines area from 1880 to 1950; Alaska was first enumerated in the 1880 federal census. Coverage for remote villages is often incomplete or delayed in early decades. Alaska was a district/territory until statehood in 1959
source_profiles.survival_notes: Coverage varies; remote communities may not have been enumerated until 1920 or later

source_profiles.repository: Library of Congress / Denominational archives / Alaska State Archives
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Presbyterian mission records from the 1880s (Sheldon Jackson era) at the Presbyterian Historical Society in Philadelphia. Russian Orthodox records for Haines area at the Library of Congress. Episcopal and other denominational records may also be relevant. Church records are critical for vital events before territorial registration in 1913

source_profiles.repository: NARA Pacific Alaska Region (Anchorage/Seattle)
source_profiles.coverage_notes: BIA records for Alaska Native communities in Haines area including census rolls, school enrollment records, allotment files, and agency correspondence. Also includes ANCSA (Alaska Native Claims Settlement Act, 1971) enrollment records at the BIA Alaska Regional Office. Essential for tracing Alaska Native family lines

source_profiles.repository: BLM General Land Office Records (online) / Alaska DNR Recorder's Office
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal land patents and homestead entries for Haines area searchable on BLM GLO Records website. source_profiles.repository: NARA St. Louis / NARA Pacific Alaska Region
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Military installation records for Haines area at NARA St. Louis and NARA Pacific Alaska Region

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Hoonah-Angoon Census Area created 2007 from the Skagway-Hoonah-Angoon Census Area. Covers communities in northern Southeast Alaska including Hoonah (largest Tlingit village), Angoon, Tenakee Springs, Gustavus, Pelican, and Elfin Cove. Glacier Bay National Park is within the census area. The 1882 U.S. Navy bombardment of Angoon is a significant historical event.
county_metadata.research_tips: Hoonah-Angoon Census Area covers communities in northern Southeast Alaska including Hoonah, Angoon, Tenakee Springs, and Elfin Cove. Tlingit peoples of the Huna and Kootznoowoo clans are central to the area's history. Russian Orthodox Church records from Angoon and surrounding areas are at the Library of Congress (microfilm). Presbyterian mission records for Hoonah from the 1880s are at the Presbyterian Historical Society in Philadelphia. Alaska State Archives holds territorial vital records from 1913 and First Judicial District court records. BIA records at NARA include school records, census rolls, and allotment files for Angoon and Hoonah. The Alaska Native Brotherhood (ANB) records at the Alaska State Library document political and community activities of Tlingit families. Sealaska Heritage Institute in Juneau holds cultural and genealogical resources. Federal census from 1880 for Southeast Alaska. The 1882 Angoon bombardment by the U.S. Navy is documented in Navy records at NARA. Hoonah-Angoon was created in 2007 from the Skagway-Hoonah-Angoon Census Area; for earlier records search under the pre-2007 designation.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM GLO Records online -- federal land patents, homestead entries, and mining claims for Hoonah-Angoon area; Alaska DNR Recorder's Office for recorded documents

county_metadata.parent_county: Unified City and Borough of Juneau formed from Greater Juneau Borough and City of Juneau
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Juneau City and Borough formed 1970 by consolidation. Alaska's state capital since 1906. Founded 1880 during the first major gold discovery in Alaska. Named for prospector Joe Juneau. The Treadwell Mine complex on Douglas Island was one of the world's largest gold mines until its collapse in 1917. Seat of territorial and state government with the richest archival collections in Alaska.
county_metadata.research_tips: Juneau City and Borough is Alaska's capital and a major hub for genealogical research. The Alaska State Archives is located in Juneau and holds territorial vital records (births/deaths from 1913, marriages from territorial period), court records from all four judicial districts, governor's papers, and territorial government files. The Alaska State Library (also in Juneau) has Alaska newspapers on microfilm, published histories, and the Alaska Historical Collections including photographs and manuscripts. First Judicial District Superior Court in Juneau handles probate for the capital area. The Juneau-Douglas City Museum holds local mining-era records (Juneau gold rush from 1880), Treadwell Mine records, and community photographs. Russian Orthodox records for Juneau area at the Library of Congress. Tlingit (Auk and Taku) family records at Sealaska Heritage Institute. Federal census from 1880 for Juneau (one of Alaska's earliest enumerated areas). BLM GLO patents and mining claims searchable online. Juneau city directories from the early 1900s at Alaska State Library. The Gastineau Channel Historical Society has mining and maritime records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM GLO Records online -- federal land patents, homestead entries, and mining claims for Juneau area; Alaska DNR Recorder's Office for recorded documents

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Kenai Peninsula Borough organized 1964. Covers the large Kenai Peninsula south of Anchorage. Russian Fort St. Nicholas (1791) at Kenai was one of the earliest European settlements in Alaska. Communities include Soldotna (borough seat), Kenai, Homer, Seward, and Seldovia. Seward was the Alaska Railroad ocean terminus. The 1964 earthquake and tsunami devastated Seward and other coastal communities. Oil and gas, fishing, and tourism are major industries.
county_metadata.research_tips: Kenai Peninsula Borough covers a large area south of Anchorage with diverse communities including Soldotna, Kenai, Homer, and Seward. Russian colonial records begin with the Russian fort at Kenai (Fort St. Nicholas/Nikolaevskii Redoubt, 1791) -- Russian Orthodox parish records for Kenai from the 1840s are at the Library of Congress (microfilm). The Holy Assumption of the Virgin Mary Church in Kenai is one of the oldest Russian Orthodox churches in Alaska. Alaska State Archives holds territorial vital records from 1913 and Third Judicial District court/probate records. Seward was the Alaska Railroad terminus, and railroad employment records are at NARA and the Alaska Railroad Corporation. Homesteading records from the 1890s-1960s are at BLM Alaska and NARA. The Kenai Peninsula Borough Clerk has administrative records from 1964. The Kenai Historical Society and Pratt Museum in Homer hold local collections. Federal census from 1880 for Kenai area. The 1964 earthquake devastated Seward -- disaster records at NARA and Alaska State Archives.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM GLO Records online -- federal land patents, homestead entries, and mining claims for Kenai Peninsula area; Alaska DNR Recorder's Office for recorded documents

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Ketchikan Gateway Borough organized 1963. Southernmost major community in Southeast Alaska, the 'First City' for northbound travelers on the Inside Passage. Tlingit and Haida peoples' homeland. Developed as a salmon cannery and timber town from the 1880s. Ketchikan Pulp Company (1954-1997) was a major employer. Saxman Totem Park and Totem Bight preserve Tlingit and Haida totem poles.
county_metadata.research_tips: Ketchikan Gateway Borough is the southernmost major community in Southeast Alaska. Tlingit and Haida peoples have long histories here, and Native clan records are at Sealaska Heritage Institute in Juneau and the Ketchikan Indian Community tribal office. Tongass Historical Museum in Ketchikan holds local family papers, photographs, fishing industry records, and timber/pulp mill employment records. Alaska State Archives has territorial vital records from 1913 and First Judicial District court/probate files. Ketchikan developed as a fishing and timber town from the 1880s -- salmon cannery employment records are scattered among company archives. BIA records at NARA include school records for the Ketchikan Industrial Training School (Sheldon Jackson era). The Totem Heritage Center documents cultural heritage. Federal census from 1880 for Ketchikan area. BLM GLO patents searchable online. Ketchikan newspapers (Ketchikan Daily News) at the Alaska State Library. The borough was organized in 1963; for earlier land records check the Ketchikan Recording District at Alaska DNR.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM GLO Records online -- federal land patents, homestead entries, and mining claims for Ketchikan Gateway area; Alaska DNR Recorder's Office for recorded documents

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Kodiak Island Borough organized 1963. Kodiak was the first capital of Russian America (1784-1808, under Alexander Baranov). Home to Alutiiq (Sugpiaq) peoples with continuous occupation for over 7,000 years. Largest U.S. Coast Guard base. Commercial fishing (salmon, crab, halibut) is the primary industry. The 1964 earthquake and tsunami devastated the town of Kodiak.
county_metadata.research_tips: Kodiak Island Borough covers Kodiak Island and nearby islands, with a deep Russian colonial history. The Russian-American Company headquarters was at Kodiak (then called Pavlovsk) from 1784 to 1808. Russian Orthodox parish records for Kodiak are among the oldest in Alaska, dating from the 1790s, and are available at the Library of Congress (Alaskan Russian Church Archives microfilm). The Baranov Museum (Kodiak Historical Society) holds local family papers, photographs, and artifacts from the Russian and American periods. Alaska State Archives has territorial vital records from 1913 and Third Judicial District court/probate files. Alutiiq (Sugpiaq) community records are at the Alutiiq Museum and Archaeological Repository in Kodiak and BIA records at NARA. Salmon cannery records (Kodiak was a major fishing center from the 1880s) are at UAF and company archives. Federal census from 1880. Military records from the Kodiak Naval Station (WWII onward) at NARA. BLM GLO land patents searchable online. The 1964 earthquake and tsunami devastated downtown Kodiak -- disaster records at NARA and Alaska State Archives.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM GLO Records online -- federal land patents, homestead entries, and mining claims for Kodiak Island area; Alaska DNR Recorder's Office for recorded documents

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Kusilvak Census Area was renamed from Wade Hampton Census Area in 2015. Covers the Yukon Delta region in western Alaska. Predominantly Yup'ik population. One of the most remote and least accessible census areas in Alaska. Communities include Hooper Bay, Emmonak, Chevak, and Mountain Village. Named for Kusilvak Mountain.
county_metadata.research_tips: Kusilvak Census Area (renamed from Wade Hampton Census Area in 2015) covers the Yukon Delta region, predominantly Yup'ik communities. For records before 2015, search under Wade Hampton Census Area. Catholic mission records from Holy Cross Mission (1888) and other Jesuit missions along the lower Yukon are at the Oregon Province Archives of the Society of Jesus at Gonzaga University. Alaska State Archives holds territorial vital records from 1913 and Second Judicial District court records. BIA records at NARA Pacific Alaska Region include census rolls, school records, and allotment files for communities in the Bethel Agency. Federal census coverage begins 1880 but is extremely limited for remote Yukon Delta villages until the 1940s. Alaska Native allotment records at BLM Alaska. The Calista Corporation (ANCSA regional corporation) in Anchorage may have shareholder genealogical records. Oral history collections at UAF include Yup'ik elders' recordings. Village council offices may hold local records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM GLO Records online -- federal land patents, homestead entries, and mining claims for Kusilvak area; Alaska DNR Recorder's Office for recorded documents

source_profiles.source_name: Kusilvak Territorial and State Vital Records -- Alaska State Archives / Bureau of Vital Statistics

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Territorial birth and death registrations for Kusilvak area from 1913; marriage records from territorial period; modern vital records from the Alaska Bureau of Vital Statistics. Pre-1913 vital events must be traced through church records, Russian Orthodox parish registers, and missionary records
source_profiles.survival_notes: Territorial records generally complete from 1913; earlier coverage varies greatly by community

source_profiles.source_name: Kusilvak Church and Mission Records
source_profiles.repository: Library of Congress / Denominational archives / Alaska State Archives

Russian Orthodox records for older communities at the Library of Congress. Church records are essential for vital events before territorial registration in 1913

source_profiles.repository: NARA Pacific Alaska Region (Anchorage/Seattle)

Also includes ANCSA (Alaska Native Claims Settlement Act, 1971) enrollment records at the BIA Alaska Regional Office. Essential for tracing Alaska Native family lines

source_profiles.source_name: Kusilvak Land Patents, Homesteads, and Mining Claims -- BLM GLO / Alaska DNR

Alaska DNR Recorder's Office holds recorded documents (deeds, mortgages) for the recording district covering Kusilvak. Mining claims (both lode and placer) at BLM Alaska State Office. Alaska was a public land state -- most land was federal domain

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Lake and Peninsula Borough organized 1989. Covers a vast area of the Alaska Peninsula and lake country. Includes Iliamna Lake (Alaska's largest), Katmai National Park, and numerous small communities. Mixed Yup'ik, Dena'ina, and Alutiiq population. King Salmon is the administrative center. The 1912 Novarupta volcanic eruption reshaped the landscape and displaced villages.
county_metadata.research_tips: Lake and Peninsula Borough covers a vast area of the Alaska Peninsula and lake country, home to Yup'ik, Dena'ina, and Alutiiq communities. Russian Orthodox mission records from the 1800s for communities along the Alaska Peninsula are at the Library of Congress (microfilm). Alaska State Archives holds territorial vital records from 1913 and Third Judicial District court records. BIA records at NARA include census rolls and school records for Bristol Bay Agency communities. Federal census from 1880 but very sparse for remote communities before 1920. The borough was organized in 1989 -- for earlier administrative records check the Third Judicial District court files. BLM GLO patents and Alaska Native allotment records at BLM Alaska. Katmai National Park records (NPS) document the 1912 Novarupta volcanic eruption and village evacuations. Cannery records from communities along the Alaska Peninsula at UAF. King Salmon is the administrative center but has limited archival holdings -- most records are in Anchorage or Juneau.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM GLO Records online -- federal land patents, homestead entries, and mining claims for Lake and Peninsula area; Alaska DNR Recorder's Office for recorded documents

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Matanuska-Susitna Borough organized 1964. Rapidly growing area north of Anchorage. Palmer was site of the 1935 Matanuska Colony, a New Deal agricultural resettlement program that brought families from the Midwest. Communities include Palmer (borough seat), Wasilla, and Big Lake. Gold mining in the Willow Creek district from 1897. Connected to Anchorage by the Glenn and Parks Highways.
county_metadata.research_tips: Matanuska-Susitna Borough north of Anchorage is one of Alaska's fastest-growing areas. The Matanuska Colony records (1935 New Deal agricultural colony) are a unique genealogical source -- colonist applications, correspondence, and photographs are at NARA Pacific Alaska Region and the Palmer Museum of History and Art. Alaska State Archives holds territorial vital records from 1913 and Third Judicial District court/probate files. Alaska Railroad records (1914+) at NARA and the Alaska Railroad Corporation document workers and communities along the rail line through the borough. Gold mining records from the Willow Creek and Hatcher Pass districts are at BLM Alaska and UAF. Dena'ina Athabascan community records at BIA NARA and Chickaloon Village tribal offices. Federal census from 1910 for the area. The Mat-Su Borough Clerk has administrative records from 1964. BLM GLO homestead patents searchable online. Palmer and Wasilla city records at respective city clerk offices. Local newspapers at Alaska State Library.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM GLO Records online -- federal land patents, homestead entries, and mining claims for Matanuska-Susitna area; Alaska DNR Recorder's Office for recorded documents

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Nome Census Area is an unorganized census area covering the Seward Peninsula and Bering Strait coast. Named for the city of Nome, site of the 1898-1900 gold rush that brought over 20,000 people to the area. Predominantly Inupiaq and Yup'ik population outside Nome. Second Judicial District seat at Nome with significant court records from 1900. The 1925 serum run to Nome (commemorated by the Iditarod) is a notable historical event.
county_metadata.research_tips: Nome Census Area covers the Seward Peninsula and Bering Strait coast, site of the famous 1898-1900 Nome gold rush. Mining claim records at BLM Alaska and NARA Pacific Alaska Region are extensive. The Carrie M. McLain Memorial Museum in Nome holds mining-era photographs, personal papers, and community records. Alaska State Archives has territorial vital records from 1913 and Second Judicial District court/probate files -- Nome was the seat of the Second Judicial District and has rich court records. Swedish Evangelical mission records and Catholic mission records for King Island and Little Diomede are at denominational archives. BIA records at NARA include census rolls and school records for Inupiaq and Yup'ik communities on the Seward Peninsula. Federal census from 1900 (the gold rush swelled Nome's population). Nome city records and newspapers (Nome Nugget from 1900) at UAF and Alaska State Library. BLM GLO patents searchable online. The Bering Straits Native Corporation (ANCSA) may have shareholder records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM GLO Records online -- federal land patents, homestead entries, and mining claims for Nome area; Alaska DNR Recorder's Office for recorded documents
